Hi Brad,

Thank you for hopping on the call today.

We discussed how Neighborly's franchise development team has been working with us to bring more science to territory design and prioritization — moving beyond simple household counts toward a more nuanced understanding of what actually drives franchisee success by trade area. On the marketing side, you raised good questions about the granularity and sourcing of the psychographic data, and we walked through how Spatial AI's Persona Live dataset differs from traditional mosaics by grounding segments in actual mobile visitation, online behavior, and transaction data rather than self-reported surveys.

The Mr. Rooter and Molly Maid customer data we've already ingested showed some clear differences between Ohio and Texas in terms of which segments are engaging — that kind of visibility could be useful as your team thinks about lead quality, churn patterns, and where certain brands resonate versus where they don't. Happy to dig deeper into any of those angles with you.

Relevant Resources

  • Customer Insights — Market Intelligence — Directly relevant to the psychographic segmentation walkthrough we did with Mr. Rooter and Molly Maid customer data, including the Ohio vs. Texas segment comparison you saw on the call.
  • Franchise Growth Webinar — Covers how franchise systems use trade area and customer data to inform development decisions — aligned with how Neighborly's franchise dev team is currently using the platform.

Thanks,
Lucas Boney

Suggested subject: Great connecting — SiteZeus resources & next steps

Hi Chad,

Thank you for hopping on the call today.

We discussed your goal of expanding from 220 to 500+ corporate-owned U.S. locations and the reality that the obvious markets are largely spoken for. With site selection currently driven by broker relationships and internal tenure, the challenge ahead is identifying where those next several hundred locations should go — with confidence — before committing to leases.

SiteZeus would build a model using your revenue or membership data alongside site characteristics like simulator bay count and co-tenancy, layered with psychographic and mobile data to pinpoint the specific customer profile that drives performance at your top locations. That gives you a proactive development roadmap — prioritized markets, not just reactive scoring when a broker brings a deal — while preserving the judgment you and the founder have built over decades as a final check.

Relevant Resources

  • SiteZeus Locate — Site Selection — Directly addresses your need to proactively identify and prioritize the next 250+ corporate locations rather than relying solely on broker-sourced opportunities.
  • White Space Analysis Guide — Relevant to your stated belief that GOLFTEC can support 500 locations — this would help frame how to systematically identify untapped markets across the U.S.
  • Customer Stories — You asked about how SiteZeus has worked with niche service brands; client stories would show comparable use cases and real outcomes.

Thanks,
Lucas Boney

We discussed your goal of building a confident, data-backed territory mapping framework for both brands — one that iterates on specific markets first before deploying across the U.S. You were clear that your first-party data (sales performance, customer zip codes from in-store purchases) should actively inform the analysis, not sit on the sideline, and I want to confirm that is exactly how our process works. Your proprietary data helps us understand variable thresholds at your best and worst sites, and your customer zip code data will directly shape how we define defensible protected territory radii rather than relying on third-party mobile data alone.

On the timeline, you can expect a 45–60 day onboarding given the two brands, with weekly or biweekly check-ins and iterative market-level sanity checks built in throughout. Hi Sam,

Thank you for hopping on the call today.

We discussed your team's goal of building a location-scoring framework that can predict enrollment potential — essentially identifying which markets and sites look like your top-performing campuses versus the ones that haven't worked. You were candid that at 18 open locations, a fully predictive model carries real limitations, and I appreciated that directness — it aligns exactly with how we think about it too.

Rather than overpromising on a statistical model at your current scale, our recommended starting point would be a deep collaborative analysis of your existing portfolio to surface what's actually driving enrollment — demographics, competitive landscape, family psychographics, and school-density patterns. That becomes the foundation for a scorecard that grades new opportunities and supports Sam's team in understanding whether a market can actually be activated through targeted marketing. We'd grow the model's predictive power as your footprint scales.

Next Steps

  • Follow-up call with Sam and Dan included — date/time not confirmed on this call, to be scheduled

Relevant Resources

  • SiteZeus Locate — Site Selection — Directly supports the location-scoring and enrollment-potential framework Justin, Sam, and Dan described needing for GM-level site decisions.
  • Customer Insights — Market Intelligence — Sam specifically called out needing clean competitive school data and understanding family demographics before launching marketing in a new market.
  • White Space Analysis Guide — Dan described wanting to evaluate which states and markets have viable untapped opportunity before committing to a location.

Thanks,
Lucas Boney

Suggested subject: Great connecting — SiteZeus resources & next steps

Hi Ryan,

Thank you for hopping on the call today.

We discussed your goal of growing from roughly 80 to 200 Wingstop locations and the core challenge standing in the way: because your business runs at roughly 60% third-party delivery, traditional white space and impact models don't fully capture whether a new site will cannibalize an existing one or open up genuinely new demand.

Our impact analysis uses mobile data to track customer behavior across existing locations and model transfer risk at potential new sites — and while we do capture some third-party delivery traffic through that mobile data, I want to be straightforward with you: we don't have a dedicated DSP radius tool, and neither does anyone else right now given how tightly Uber Eats and DoorDash guard that routing data. That said, if your team ever wants to see how our impact and forecasting models perform against your existing predictive model in a market like Detroit or Long Island, I'm happy to set up a deeper demo.

Relevant Resources

  • Sales Impact & Cannibalization — Ryan's primary concern was understanding delivery-driven cannibalization when opening stores in dense markets like Queens and Long Island — this speaks directly to that challenge.
  • Customer Insights — Market Intelligence — Ryan is already using mobile data and Placer; this shows how SiteZeus's mobile data sourcing compares and how it informs trade area analysis in dense, delivery-heavy markets.

Thanks,
Emma Keeney

Hi Tyler,

Below is a summary of the action items discussed during Daily Sync - SiteZeus | Fresh Kitchen.

Quick Call Review

  • Reviewed Fresh Kitchen's customer profile using 90K uploaded records — strong indexing on Upper Suburban Diverse Families (2x likelihood) and Blue Collar Suburbs, with Educated Urbanites flagged as underrepresented in the upload but confirmed present via mobile data.
  • Aligned on a scorecard weighting approach: 50% customer profile segments, 50% demographic data (split evenly between total population and daytime pop to reflect the balanced lunch/dinner mix).
  • Cava used as a proxy for credit card spend benchmarking given Fresh Kitchen isn't yet in the dataset — useful for validating segment direction and identifying upside with Wealthy Suburban Families and Sunset Boomers.
  • Identified an opportunity to run store-level customer profile uploads using the 'most frequent store' variable already in the data — would enable more granular market analysis.

Action Items

SiteZeus Team (Aaron Bergland)

  • Schedule and conduct full platform training session for the Fresh Kitchen team after the current white space deliverable is complete.
  • Complete white space analysis by the agreed deadline, accounting for the office closure on Juneteenth (Friday).

Customer Team (Tyler Azzarelli)

  • Consider re-uploading customer data segmented by store (using the 'most frequent store' variable) to enable location-level profile analysis.

Key Decisions

  • Scorecard will weight customer profile segments at 50% and demographic data at 50%, with total population and daytime population split evenly within the demographic half.
  • Educated Urbanites will be kept in the customer profile despite low representation in the uploaded dataset, supported by mobile data and strategic importance for target expansion markets.

Next Milestone

  • White space analysis to be delivered by end of this week, with full team training to follow once that deliverable is complete.

Best,
Aaron Bergland

Based on where you are today, the honest fit is a bit early for a full SiteZeus subscription, since our model works best when tied directly to a brand with existing location data. That said, the path that tends to work well for consultants in your position is bringing SiteZeus in as the brand's vendor — they hold the contract, you get access, and the data stays tied to their entity. As you build out those brand relationships, I am happy to stay in touch and revisit what a tailored setup could look like. I will also keep you posted if we introduce any usage-based options down the road.
